skill.name: journal-latest-issue
skill.description: Retrieve the latest journal issue's table of contents and abstracts from URL/DOI/PMID/RSS/TOC sources, then generate Chinese key points locally (no external translation APIs) when a new issue needs quick review and archiving.
skill.summary: Fetch latest journal issue TOC and abstracts from RSS, DOI, PubMed, and TOC sources. Export structured digests to Markdown and CSV for quick research review.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 68 potential issues: 14 HIGH (weak crypto), 33 MEDIUM (shell backtick), and 21 LOW (network/hardcoded URLs). All HIGH findings are false positives from keyword substring matches (e.g., 'desc' in URL parameters, 'description' in HTML meta tags). MEDIUM findings are markdown documentation code blocks, not executable shell commands. The skill makes legitimate HTTP requests to academic APIs (Crossref, PubMed, Nature) with an enforced domain allowlist and writes local output files. No malicious intent, obfuscation, or data exfiltration patterns detected. No prompt injection found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Network access to academic APIs
low_findings.description: The skill makes HTTP requests to Crossref, PubMed, Nature, and ScienceDirect APIs and RSS feeds using Python urllib. This is expected behavior for a journal digest tool. A domain allowlist restricts connections to known academic hosts.

low_findings.title: Filesystem write access
low_findings.description: The skill writes output files (Markdown and CSV) to the local filesystem. This is expected behavior for generating structured digests. Only output files are written, no existing files are modified.

content.value_statement: Researchers spend hours manually checking journal websites for new issues. This skill automates the process by fetching tables of contents and abstracts from any journal using RSS, DOI, PubMed, or TOC URLs, then generates a structured digest for quick review.
actual_capabilities: Fetch latest issue table of contents from RSS feeds and TOC URLs
Retrieve article abstracts from journal landing pages and PubMed
Support single journal and batch mode via JSON configuration file
Export structured digests to Markdown and CSV output formats
Look up article metadata through Crossref and PubMed API services
Enforce domain allowlist to restrict web crawling to known academic hosts
limitations: Cannot access paywalled or subscription-only article full text
Chinese key points are generated locally and are not professional translation
Needs network access to journal websites and academic databases
Abstract retrieval depends on availability of open metadata from publishers

use_cases.title: Weekly Journal Scanning for Researchers
use_cases.description: A PhD student tracks 5 journals in their field and runs this skill every week to get the latest issue TOC and abstracts compiled into a single Markdown file for efficient scanning.

use_cases.title: Literature Review Preparation
use_cases.description: A medical librarian uses batch mode to collect article metadata from 10 or more journals and exports to CSV for systematic review screening and reference management.
use_cases.target_user: Medical librarians and systematic review teams

use_cases.title: Research Group Digest Sharing
use_cases.description: A lab principal investigator runs the skill for key journals in their specialty and shares the output Markdown file with the research group for weekly journal discussion meetings.
use_cases.target_user: Research group leaders and laboratory managers

prompt_templates.title: Single Journal Quick Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch the latest issue from the journal Nature using the RSS feed and show me the first 5 articles with their titles and DOIs.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants a quick overview of the newest articles in a specific journal

prompt_templates.title: Batch Mode with JSON Config
prompt_templates.prompt: Load my batch configuration from journals.json, process all 8 journals listed in it, and save the output to output.md and output.csv. Fetch abstracts for all articles.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a pre-configured JSON file with multiple journals and sources

prompt_templates.title: Cross-Reference Abstract Lookup
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch articles from the Nature RSS feed. For any articles missing abstracts, look them up first on Crossref and then on PubMed to get the best coverage.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants comprehensive abstract coverage by querying multiple databases

prompt_templates.title: Selective Abstract Refetch
prompt_templates.prompt: Process my input CSV of previously saved articles. Re-fetch abstracts only for items 1, 3, and 5 using their DOI links. Update the digest files with the new data.
prompt_templates.scenario: User previously collected articles and wants to selectively enrich specific entries

output_examples.input: Fetch the latest Nature issue with 3 articles including abstracts.
output_examples.output:
# A quick overview of the current issue of the journal

## Nature
- serial number: 1
  - title: Climate change impacts on biodiversity
  - DOI: 10.1038/s41586-024-xxxxx
  - URL: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-xxxxx
  - summary: [Abstract text appears here]
  - Chinese essentials: Chinese essentials（untranslated）：Climate change impacts on biodiversity。Summary excerpt：[First 160 characters of abstract]...

[Additional articles follow the same format]

output_examples.input: Process 2 journals in batch mode with CSV export.
output_examples.output:
Two files are generated:
1. output.md - A Markdown document with articles grouped by journal (Nature and Cell), each showing serial number, title, DOI, URL, abstract, and Chinese key points.
2. output.csv - A CSV table with columns: index, journal, title, doi, url, abstract, summary_zh. Ready for import into spreadsheet or reference management software.

output_examples.input: Re-fetch abstracts for items 2 and 4 from an existing CSV.
output_examples.output: The updated output.md now shows complete abstracts for articles 2 and 4 (previously missing). Their Chinese key points have been regenerated with the full abstract content. Other articles retain their original data. Console prints: DONE

best_practices: Always provide RSS or TOC URLs for the most reliable and up-to-date results rather than relying on automatic source detection
Use the built-in domain allowlist and avoid --allow-all unless you explicitly trust the target website
Start with --max-items 10 to test output format and data quality before running full batch jobs
anti_patterns: Using --allow-all on untrusted or unknown journal websites bypasses the domain allowlist and increases security risk
Expecting the skill to access paywalled or subscription-only content that the user does not have access to through their institution
Relying on the locally generated Chinese key points as a substitute for professional translation or verified interpretation

faq.question: What sources can this skill fetch from?
faq.answer: It supports RSS feeds, TOC URLs, journal names with Crossref search, PubMed ID lookups, and DOI lookups.

faq.question: Does this skill require third-party Python packages?
faq.answer: No, it uses only the Python standard library. No pip install or external dependencies are needed.

faq.question: Can I process multiple journals at once?
faq.answer: Yes. Use batch mode with a JSON configuration file that lists all journals along with their RSS or TOC sources.

faq.question: What output formats are supported?
faq.answer: The skill produces Markdown (.md) for human reading and CSV for data processing and spreadsheet import.

faq.question: How are the Chinese key points generated?
faq.answer: They are generated locally from the English title and abstract text. No external translation APIs are called.

faq.question: Is the domain allowlist enforced by default?
faq.answer: Yes. Only known academic hosts are allowed by default. Use the --allow-all flag only when you trust the target domain.
